what: R Package (mhurtado13/racing) vs. Original Python Implementation (SysBioOncology/RaCInG)
title: RaCInG: Detailed Comparison Report
text: Package documentation: https://mhurtado13.github.io/racing/

title: RaCInG: Detailed Comparison Report
text: R package RaCInG builds upon original Python-based RaCInG pipeline, repackaging distributable R package. original implementation established core mathematical framework—random graph generation, kernel-based feature computation, Monte Carlo simulation, graph motif extraction—R package faithfully preserves adapting architecture R-native workflows package distribution. report documents every change, adaptation, addition detail.

title: RaCInG: Detailed Comparison Report
text: entire codebase rewritten Python R. Key translation decisions include: NumPy arrays → R matrices array() 3D/4D structures SciPy sparse matrices → Matrix::sparseMatrix (dgCMatrix) Pandas DataFrames → R data.frame dplyr/tibble manipulation Python classes (informationMicroEnv) → Standalone R functions explicit parameter passing Python csv module → utils::read.csv() data.table::fread() Matplotlib plotting → ggplot2-based plotting

title: RaCInG: Detailed Comparison Report
text: Defines class informationMicroEnv encapsulates cell-type distributions, ligand-receptor distributions, connectivity rules. Class methods: genRandomEdgeList(), genRandomCellTypeList() Imports distribution_generation module (separate file) genRandomCellTypeDistr, genRandomLigRecDistr, genRandomCellLigands, genRandomCellReceptors. model1() function creates informationMicroEnv instance generates graph.

title: RaCInG: Detailed Comparison Report
text: Adapted class-based functional design: informationMicroEnv class restructured standalone functions (genRandomCellTypeDistr(), genRandomLigRecDistr(), genRandomCellLigands(), genRandomCellReceptors(), genRandomCellTypeList(), genRandomEdgeList()), independently documented exported. Consolidated distribution_generation file: separate Python module distribution_generation.py (referenced import distribution_generation dg) incorporated directly network_generation.R, keeping network generation logic one place. functions individually documented roxygen2 @param, @return, @export tags. model1(): Converted using class instance calling standalone functions. Parameters use R-style NULL defaults instead Python empty lists []. Added generateUniformLRGraph(): new exported function generates graph uniformized (flat) LR distribution specific patient. function exist original Python code useful normalization workflows. 1-based indexing: vertex cell-type indices use R’s 1-based convention vs. Python’s 0-based.

title: RaCInG: Detailed Comparison Report
text: Contains graph motif counting functions: - Find_Number_Trust_Triangles_Unique(), Find_Number_Triangles(), Find_Number_Triangles_Unique() - Find_Number_2Loops(), Find_Number_2Loops_Unique() - Find_Number_Wedges(), Find_Number_Wedges_Unique() - Trust_Triangles(), Cycle_Triangles(), Wedges() - BFS(), StrongConnect(), Tarjan(), TarjanIterative(), sconnect() - GSCC(), (), () Uses scipy.sparse matrix operations. Contains recursive iterative Tarjan implementations.

title: RaCInG: Detailed Comparison Report
text: functions faithfully ported identical algorithmic logic. Dense matrix operations default: Python used scipy.sparse .sign(), .getrow(), .nonzero(), R version uses standard matrix indexing (Adj[v, ], (Adj[v, ] != 0)). simpler may less memory-efficient large graphs. adjacency matrix construction Utilities.R use Matrix::sparseMatrix, feature extraction functions operate result using standard R matrix operations. Tarjan’s algorithm: iterative version (TarjanIterative) ported. recursive Tarjan() / StrongConnect() Python dropped, avoiding R’s recursion depth limitations large graphs. iterative version uses R closures (<<- assignment) manage state, replacing Python’s mutable list/dict references. Removed recursive Tarjan()/StrongConnect() functions entirely. iterative-approach robust large graphs R. Added detailed inline comments explaining mathematics behind motif counting operation.

title: RaCInG: Detailed Comparison Report
text: Calculate_kernel(): Reads input files disk (hardcoded naming: {cancer}_TMEmod_cell_fractions.csv), computes kernels, saves .npz files. saveKernel(): Saves kernel disk compressed NumPy arrays (.npz). calculateDirect(), calculateWedges(): Extract features kernels write CSV files disk (naming convention: {cancer}_{weight}_weight_Dir_bundle_norm.csv). getGSCCAnalytically(): Computes GSCC analytically using nleqslv-equivalent SciPy, reads patient data files. Heavy reliance file /O throughout; tightly coupled specific directory structure.

title: RaCInG: Detailed Comparison Report
text: compute_kernel() replaces Calculate_kernel(): Accepts matrices function arguments instead reading files disk. file /O inside function—pure computation. Supports optional normalize parameter also return uniformized baseline kernel call. calculateDirect() calculateWedges(): Rewritten take kernel arrays cell-name vectors arguments return data frames. file writing. bundle parameter combining reciprocal directions preserved. computeGSCC() replaces getGSCCAnalytically(): mathematical approach (Poisson branching process via nleqslv), receives kernel abundance matrices arguments rather reading files. poiBPFunc() helper preserved internal function. getGSCCAnalytically() retained legacy stub: original function name kept now stop()s message directing users computeGSCC(), ensuring backward-compatible error messages. NEW: computeTriangles(): Computes triangle features (trust triangles bundled cycle triangles) directly kernel arrays. feature extraction function direct equivalent original Python Kernel_Method.py—Python version computed triangles via Monte Carlo simulation. NEW: compute_kernel_features(): high-level dispatcher routes calculateDirect(), calculateWedges(), computeTriangles(), computeGSCC() based communication_type parameter. Supports patient subsetting via patient_idx. function simplifies user API. Accepts either raw count matrices pre-computed input data Calls prepare_input_files() (needed) Calls compute_kernel() optional normalization Calls compute_kernel_features() requested feature type Returns kernel arrays feature data frame -memory kernel handling: Kernels returned R arrays instead saved disk .npz, enabling interactive exploration. Flexible file naming: assumptions cancer type names, folder structures, file naming conventions, allowing use dataset.

title: RaCInG: Detailed Comparison Report
text: countWedges(), countTrustTriangles(), countCycleTriangles(), countDirect(), countGSCC(): Functions run Monte Carlo simulations count graph motifs. runSimOne(): Runs simulation single patient. runSim(): Iterates patients, writes results structured .txt/.files well-defined layout. Output uses purpose-built text format, companion parsers txt_to_csv.py. Provides console output progress tracking.

title: RaCInG: Detailed Comparison Report
text: Core counting functions preserved: countWedges(), countTrustTriangles(), countCycleTriangles(), countDirect(), countGSCC() faithfully replicate Python logic R syntax. runSimOne() removed (commented ): per-patient function replaced loop inside runSim() directly. commented-code preserved reference. Added output_folder parameter automatic directory creation. Added file.name parameter customizable output naming. Added patient_idx parameter allowing simulation single specific patient without re-running patients. Uses R file() connections writing (proper resource management via con). Output format structured CSV-like, compatible Read_Sim_Output(). Accepts raw counts pre-computed input data Optionally generates input files Calls runSim() requested feature type Reads back results returns R data structures

title: RaCInG: Detailed Comparison Report
text: sortPermute(): Sorts list returns permutation. createCellLigList(), createCellRecList(), createCellTypeDistr(), createInteractionDistr(): Read CSV files following consistent naming convention (e.g., {cancer}_celltype_ligand_{weight}.csv). Read_Lig_Rec_Interaction(): Reads ligand-receptor interaction file. generateInput(): Master function calls readers hardcoded file naming. get_patient_names(): Extracts patient names file names.

title: RaCInG: Detailed Comparison Report
text: reader functions ported generalized: createCellLigList(), createCellRecList(), createCellTypeDistr(), createInteractionDistr() accept explicit filename parameters rather constructing paths cancer type weight type. makes usable naming convention. createCellTypeDistr() enhanced: Includes M1/M2 macrophage merging logic done externally original Python workflow. function now accepts cells parameter automatically merges M1+M2 number cell types doesn’t match. createInteractionDistr() enhanced: Builds proper 3D tensor (ligand × receptor × patient) instead flat matrix. Includes automatic normalization patient’s interaction distribution sums 1. Handles NA values replacing 0. sortPermute() preserved internal helper. generateInput() generalized: Uses file_name output_folder parameters instead cancer type/weight type. Supports optional read_signs parameter. get_patient_names() removed: needed patient names extracted directly data frame row names R. Normalizes counts TPM (via ADImpute::NormalizeTPM) Runs deconvolution (via multideconv) accepts pre-computed estimates Estimates cell-type expression profiles Retrieves ligand-receptor prior knowledge OmniPath/LIANA (accepts custom network) Filters interactions expression threshold (≥10 TPM) Builds cell-cell communication table Computes four input matrices (Lmatrix, Rmatrix, Cmatrix, LRmatrix) Exports CSV files Returns processed matrices ready analysis

title: RaCInG: Detailed Comparison Report
text: EdgetoAdj(): Builds sparse matrix edge list. EdgetoAdj_No_loop(): removes self-loops. Count_Types(): Counts motifs cell-type combinations. createSlurm(): Generates SLURM job submission scripts HPC. poiBPFunc(): Poisson branching process helper GSCC computation.

title: RaCInG: Detailed Comparison Report
text: EdgetoAdj() EdgetoAdj_No_loop() ported: Use Matrix::sparseMatrix() instead scipy.sparse. functions exported documented. Count_Types() ported generalized: Now works R’s 1-based indexing. Accepts optional maxTypes parameter. poiBPFunc() preserved internal helper. createSlurm() done yet: implemented R using parallelization frameworks (parallel, future, batchtools) users need HPC capabilities.

title: RaCInG: Detailed Comparison Report
text: Comprehensive file (~800+ lines) providing full downstream analysis suite: - remove_celltype(), readAllDataExact(), dataReadExact(), data_read(): Data reading/preprocessing functions tailored original pipeline’s file structure. - Find_Patient_Subtype_Bagaev(): Reads metadata Excel file (Bagaev_mmc6.xlsx). - Metadata_csv_read_in(): Reads metadata CSV files. - readAllData(): Reads Monte Carlo output CSVs. - addMetadata(), removeOutliers(): Data cleaning functions. - computeCorrelation(): Spearman correlation features immune response. - groupPatients(), createPanCancerData(): Patient grouping comparisons. - wilcoxon(): Full Wilcoxon rank-sum test Bonferroni correction, pan-cancer analysis. - volcanoPan(), volcanoInd(), volcanoCross(): Matplotlib-based volcano plots (~300 lines). - findLargeFold(), plot_heatmap(), heatmap(): Fold-change analysis heatmaps. - contributionAnalysisGSCC(): GSCC contribution analysis Mann-Whitney tests.

title: RaCInG: Detailed Comparison Report
text: wilcox_group_test(): generic Wilcoxon rank-sum test function accepts data matrix group labels. Uses stats::p.adjust() multiple testing correction (supports FDR, Bonferroni, others via parameter). Returns tidy data frame sorted adjusted p-value. generalizes original’s cancer-specific testing pipeline dataset-agnostic tool. volcano_plot(): ggplot2-based volcano plot accepts output wilcox_group_test(). Data reading functions (readAllDataExact, dataReadExact, data_read, readAllData): Superseded generic input pipeline. Find_Patient_Subtype_Bagaev(): Designed specific metadata source (Bagaev_mmc6.xlsx); users can implement study-specific metadata loaders needed. Metadata_csv_read_in(): Standard CSV reading natively supported R via read.csv(). addMetadata(), removeOutliers(): Data cleaning operations well-served R’s existing ecosystem (dplyr, tidyr). groupPatients(), createPanCancerData(): Study-specific grouping logic users can implement based particular study design. computeCorrelation(): Correlation analysis well-supported natively R via cor.test() related functions. findLargeFold(), plot_heatmap(), heatmap(): Mature R packages like ComplexHeatmap pheatmap offer extensive heatmap capabilities. contributionAnalysisGSCC(): Study-specific analysis users can implement using computeGSCC() output. volcanoCross(), volcanoPan(), volcanoInd(): Consolidated single generic volcano_plot().

title: RaCInG: Detailed Comparison Report
text: Triangle_Prop_Read(): Parses custom .txt output files triangle data. Direct_Comm_Read(): Parses direct communication .txt files. GSCC_Read(): Parses GSCC .txt files. Generate_normalised_count_csv(): Reads raw simulations produces normalized CSV files. Includes direction bundling logic.

title: RaCInG: Detailed Comparison Report
text: Read_Sim_Output() unifies three readers: single function auto-detects communication type (D, W, TT, CT, GSCC) file header adapts parsing logic accordingly. original Python version used three dedicated parsers, optimized respective data dimensionality (1D GSCC, 2D Direct, 3D triangles/wedges). Generate_normalised_count_csv() included compute_results_processing(): Handles normalization Monte Carlo output, direction bundling, CSV export. Output format adapted: R package writes labeled CSV-like format runSim(), building original’s structured text format added column headers self-describing output.

title: RaCInG: Detailed Comparison Report
text: R package includes pre-processed SKCM (skin cutaneous melanoma) dataset built-package data (data/skcm_example.rda). allows users immediately run RaCInG without downloading preparing external data: original Python code provided example input files Example input/ part formal data distribution system.

title: RaCInG: Detailed Comparison Report
text: Two new high-level entry points exist original: compute_racing_kernel(): raw counts → input preparation → kernel computation → feature extraction one call. compute_racing_montecarlo(): raw counts → input preparation → Monte Carlo simulation → result processing one call. functions support “scratch” workflows (providing count matrix) “bring data” workflows (providing input_data list).

title: RaCInG: Detailed Comparison Report
text: new function acts single entry point computing type kernel-derived feature (Direct, Wedges, Trust Triangles, GSCC), patient subsetting support.

title: RaCInG: Detailed Comparison Report
text: function computeTriangles() computes triangle features analytically kernel, available via Monte Carlo simulation original Python code. eliminates need computationally expensive graph simulations triangle features needed.

title: RaCInG: Detailed Comparison Report
text: new helper function generating graphs uniform ligand-receptor distribution. useful normalization studies understanding null model.

title: RaCInG: Detailed Comparison Report
text: full documentation website built pkgdown (Bootstrap 5), available https://mhurtado13.github.io/racing/, including: - Getting Started vignette step--step walkthrough - Input files explanation section

title: RaCInG: Detailed Comparison Report
text: prepare_input_files() function uses .check_installed_packages() verify optional dependencies (ADImpute, multideconv, OmnipathR, liana) installed attempting use , providing clear error messages instead cryptic import failures.

title: RaCInG: Detailed Comparison Report
text: original Python code integrated computation file /O, well-suited batch processing workflow: - Calculate_kernel() reads CSV files, computes kernels, writes .npz files. - calculateDirect() computes features writes CSV files. - runSim() computes features writes .txt files. R package separates concerns: - Computation functions (compute_kernel(), calculateDirect(), etc.) pure: accept data arguments return results. - /O functions (prepare_input_files(), Read_Sim_Output()) handle file operations explicitly. - Workflow functions (compute_racing_kernel(), compute_racing_montecarlo()) compose computation /O.

title: RaCInG: Detailed Comparison Report
text: original Python code uses concise parameter names suited script-based workflow: - cellLigList vs structurelig, Dcelltype vs Dligrec - N (cells per graph), itNo (iterations), av (average degree) R package maintains backward compatibility original parameter names possible adding descriptive roxygen2 documentation parameter.

title: RaCInG: Detailed Comparison Report
text: compute_racing_kernel() compute_racing_montecarlo() functions accept either: 1. Raw count matrices (triggering full preprocessing pipeline) 2. Pre-computed input data lists (bypassing preprocessing) dual-mode design accommodates new users existing preprocessed data.

title: RaCInG: Detailed Comparison Report
text: step uses disk /O, providing structured reproducible file-based pipeline.
code: CSV files on disk   → generateInput() [reads files with hardcoded names]   → model1() [generates graph]   → feature extraction functions   → runSim() [writes .txt files]   → txt_to_csv functions [reads .txt, writes .csv]   → statistical_analysis functions [reads .csv, writes plots]

title: RaCInG: Detailed Comparison Report
text: Minimal disk /O. Data stays memory throughout analysis.
code: Gene count matrix (or pre-computed input_data list)   → compute_racing_kernel()     → prepare_input_files() [optional; one-time I/O]     → compute_kernel() [in-memory]     → compute_kernel_features() [in-memory]   → returns list(kernel, kernel_norm, features)

title: RaCInG: Detailed Comparison Report
text: File /O Monte Carlo output persistence (necessary due simulation size).
code: Gene count matrix (or pre-computed input_data list)   → compute_racing_montecarlo()     → prepare_input_files() [optional]     → runSim() [writes .out file]     → Read_Sim_Output() + compute_results_processing()   → returns processed results

title: RaCInG: Detailed Comparison Report
text: R package builds solid mathematical foundation established original Python implementation, adapting R ecosystem: Packaging: Organized formal, installable R package automated documentation pkgdown website. Usability: Introduced end--end workflow functions streamline multi-step analyses single calls. Generality: Parameterized file naming input handling, making package applicable dataset without modification. Separation concerns: Separated computation /O, enabling interactive exploration batch processing. New capabilities: Kernel-based triangle computation, unified feature dispatcher, bundled example data, end--end workflow functions. Focused scope: Downstream statistical analysis distilled two generic, reusable functions, leveraging R’s rich existing ecosystem specialized tasks (completed additional functions). core mathematics (random graph generation, kernel computation, motif counting, branching-process GSCC) preserved faithfully original, software engineering adapted R-native workflows package distribution.

title: Getting started with RaCInG
text: RaCInG reconstructs patient-specific cell-cell communication networks bulk RNA-seq data. package supports two complementary workflows: kernel-based approach fast deterministic feature extraction, Monte Carlo approach simulation-based network summaries. vignette shows recommended workflow important entry points new users.

title: Getting started with RaCInG
Getting started with RaCInG
text: want build RaCInG input matrices directly raw counts, install optional preprocessing dependencies used prepare_input_files():
code: # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install(".") library(RaCInG) install.packages(c("ggplot2", "OmnipathR")) # Additional optional packages: ADImpute, multideconv, liana

what: 1. Build input matrices from raw counts
title: Getting started with RaCInG
text: Use prepare_input_files() generate, save, load input matrices single call.
code: input <- prepare_input_files(   counts = counts_matrix,   output_folder = "Results/",   file_name = "SKCM" )  str(input)

title: Getting started with RaCInG
text: kernel method fastest way derive direct, wedge, triangle, GSCC features across patients. can pass counts let function compute inputs automatically, supply previously computed matrices via input_data.
code: # Option A: from raw counts (runs prepare_input_files internally) kernel_res <- compute_racing_kernel(   counts = counts_matrix,   output_folder = tempdir(),   file_name = "SKCM",   communication_type = "W",   norm = TRUE )  # Option B: from pre-computed input matrices (skips preprocessing) kernel_res <- compute_racing_kernel(   input_data = input,   communication_type = "W",   norm = TRUE )  head(kernel_res$features[, 1:5])

title: Getting started with RaCInG
text: Use Monte Carlo workflow want simulation-based summaries uncertainty estimates repeated graph realizations. input_data shortcut available .
code: set.seed(1) mc_res <- compute_racing_montecarlo(   counts = counts_matrix,   output_folder = tempdir(),   deconv_method = "Quantiseq",   file_name = "SKCM",   nPatients = 3,   communication_type = "W",   Ncells = 100,   Ngraphs = 10,   Ndegree = 3,   norm = TRUE )  # Or from pre-computed inputs: mc_res <- compute_racing_montecarlo(   input_data = input,   output_folder = tempdir(),   file_name = "SKCM",   communication_type = "W",   Ncells = 100,   Ngraphs = 10,   Ndegree = 3,   norm = TRUE )  head(mc_res$output$mean[, 1:5])

title: Getting started with RaCInG
text: features available patient--feature matrix, use built-Wilcoxon workflow compare groups.
code: grouping <- c("Responder", "Responder", "Non-responder", "Non-responder") wilcox_results <- wilcox_group_test(kernel_res$features, grouping) head(wilcox_results) volcano_plot(wilcox_results, top_labels = 15)

title: Getting started with RaCInG
text: compute_racing_kernel() compute_racing_montecarlo() main entry points. accept input_data argument pre-computed matrices (returned prepare_input_files()), skips preprocessing. prepare_input_files() requires extra packages deconvolution prior-network assembly. original Python implementation available https://github.com/SysBioOncology/RaCInG.

title: Getting started with RaCInG
text: RaCInG requires four matrices associated label vectors describe cell-cell communication landscape cohort patients. table summarises component: inputs typically generated prepare_input_files() raw counts matrix, can assembled manually pre-existing deconvolution prior-network data. bundled skcm_example dataset provides ready-made example structure.

title: RaCInG
text: RaCInG (Random Cell-cell Interaction Generator) reconstructs patient-specific cell-cell communication networks bulk RNA-seq data extracts network-level features using either kernel-based Monte Carlo workflow. random cell-cell interaction generator ## Installation

title: RaCInG
RaCInG
RaCInG
text: want start directly raw counts prepare_input_files(), install optional helper packages used deconvolution prior-network construction:
code: install.packages(c("ggplot2", "OmnipathR")) # Additional optional packages used by the full preprocessing workflow: # ADImpute, multideconv, liana

title: RaCInG
code: library(RaCInG)  # Build input matrices from raw counts input <- prepare_input_files(   counts = counts_matrix,   output_folder = "Results/",   file_name = "example" )  # Run kernel method (from raw counts) kernel_res <- compute_racing_kernel(   counts = counts_matrix,   file_name = "example",   output_folder = tempdir(),   communication_type = "W" )  # Or pass pre-computed inputs to skip preprocessing kernel_res <- compute_racing_kernel(   input_data = input,   communication_type = "W" )  # Monte Carlo method mc_res <- compute_racing_montecarlo(   input_data = input,   file_name = "example",   output_folder = tempdir(),   communication_type = "W",   Ncells = 100,   Ngraphs = 10,   Ndegree = 3 )

what: RaCInG: Random Cell-cell Interaction Generator — RaCInG-package
title: RaCInG: Random Cell-cell Interaction Generator — RaCInG-package
text: RaCInG provides functions prepare ligand-receptor input matrices, generate patient-specific communication graphs, derive kernel Monte Carlo features, perform downstream statistical analysis.

text: Maintainer: Marcelo Hurtado marcelo.hurt137@gmail.com

title: Run the full kernel-based RaCInG workflow — compute_racing_kernel
code: compute_racing_kernel(   counts = NULL,   output_folder = "~/Documents/racing/vignettes/",   deconv = NULL,   cc_network = NULL,   fun_LR = min,   cell_expr_profile = NULL,   source = "source_genesymbol",   target = "target_genesymbol",   signed = FALSE,   deconv_method = "Quantiseq",   cbsx.name = NULL,   cbsx.token = NULL,   file_name = NULL,   nPatients = "all",   communication_type = "W",   norm = TRUE,   pt_idx = NULL,   remove_direction = TRUE,   input_data = NULL )

title: Run the full kernel-based RaCInG workflow — compute_racing_kernel
text: counts Gene--sample count matrix. Required input_data supplied; ignored otherwise. output_folder Directory used write read intermediate input files. deconv Optional deconvolution matrix. cc_network Optional ligand-receptor prior network. fun_LR Function used combine ligand receptor expression values. cell_expr_profile Optional cell-type expression profile matrix. source, target Column names use ligand receptor identifiers cc_network. signed Logical; TRUE, also try load sign matrix. deconv_method Deconvolution method used deconv supplied. cbsx.name, cbsx.token Optional credentials deconvolution workflow. file_name File stem used intermediate input files. nPatients Number patients process, "". communication_type Feature family compute. norm Logical; TRUE, compute normalized baseline kernel. pt_idx Optional single patient index process. remove_direction Logical; TRUE, merge directionally equivalent features. input_data Optional named list pre-computed input matrices returned prepare_input_files(). Must contain Lmatrix, Rmatrix, Cmatrix, LRmatrix, celltypes, ligands, receptors. supplied, counts argument preprocessing parameters (deconv, cc_network, etc.) ignored.

title: Run the full kernel-based RaCInG workflow — compute_racing_kernel
text: list kernel arrays derived feature matrix.

code: compute_racing_montecarlo(   counts = NULL,   output_folder = "~/Documents/racing/vignettes/",   deconv = NULL,   cc_network = NULL,   fun_LR = min,   cell_expr_profile = NULL,   source = "source_genesymbol",   target = "target_genesymbol",   signed = FALSE,   deconv_method = "Quantiseq",   cbsx.name = NULL,   cbsx.token = NULL,   pt_idx = NULL,   file_name = NULL,   nPatients = "all",   communication_type = "W",   Ncells = 10000,   Ngraphs = 100,   Ndegree = 20,   remove_direction = TRUE,   norm = TRUE,   input_data = NULL )

title: Run the full Monte Carlo RaCInG workflow — compute_racing_montecarlo
text: counts Gene--sample count matrix. Required input_data supplied; ignored otherwise. output_folder Directory used write intermediate output files. deconv Optional deconvolution matrix. cc_network Optional ligand-receptor prior network. fun_LR Function used combine ligand receptor expression values. cell_expr_profile Optional cell-type expression profile matrix. source, target Column names use ligand receptor identifiers cc_network. signed Logical; TRUE, also try load sign matrix. deconv_method Deconvolution method used deconv supplied. cbsx.name, cbsx.token Optional credentials deconvolution workflow. pt_idx Optional single patient index simulate. file_name File stem used intermediate files. nPatients Number patients process, "". communication_type Feature family simulate. Ncells Number cells per simulated graph. Ngraphs Number Monte Carlo iterations. Ndegree Target average degree. remove_direction Logical; TRUE, merge directionally equivalent features. norm Logical; TRUE, also run uniformized baseline simulation normalization. input_data Optional named list pre-computed input matrices returned prepare_input_files(). Must contain Lmatrix, Rmatrix, Cmatrix, LRmatrix, celltypes, ligands, receptors. supplied, counts argument preprocessing parameters (deconv, cc_network, etc.) ignored.

title: Run the full Monte Carlo RaCInG workflow — compute_racing_montecarlo
text: list generated inputs processed feature matrices.

what: SKCM melanoma example input for RaCInG — skcm_example
title: SKCM melanoma example input for RaCInG — skcm_example
text: Pre-processed input matrices derived TCGA skin cutaneous melanoma (SKCM) bulk RNA-seq data. object named list can passed directly compute_racing_kernel() compute_racing_montecarlo() via input_data parameter.

title: SKCM melanoma example input for RaCInG — skcm_example
SKCM melanoma example input for RaCInG — skcm_example
text: named list following elements: Lmatrix Numeric matrix (9 cell types x 276 ligands). Binary cell--ligand compatibility. Rmatrix Numeric matrix (9 cell types x 298 receptors). Binary cell--receptor compatibility. Cmatrix Numeric matrix (10 patients x 9 cell types). Row-normalised cell-type abundance estimates deconvolution, M1 M2 macrophages merged single M category. LRmatrix 3-D numeric array (276 ligands x 298 receptors x 10 patients). Normalised ligand–receptor interaction probability tensor. celltypes Character vector 9 cell-type names (alphabetically sorted). ligands Character vector 276 ligand names. receptors Character vector 298 receptor names. Sign_matrix Numeric matrix (276 x 298) zeros (unknown interaction signs).

title: SKCM melanoma example input for RaCInG — skcm_example
text: Derived TCGA SKCM data processed TMEmod deconvolution OmniPath ligand–receptor annotations.
